# Introduction

The rise of Python in the hardware verification domain, particularly through frameworks like cocotb (coroutine-based co-simulation testbench), presents a modern alternative to conventional verification workflows.
The cocotb framework allows engineers to write testbenches in Python while interfacing directly with HDL simulators. It brings advantages like high-level abstraction, easier scripting, and integration with powerful Python libraries. Furthermore, cocotb supports coroutine-based event-driven simulations, enabling concise and readable verification code.
In parallel, the Gatemate FPGA Evaluation Board developed by Cologne Chip AG offers a cost-effective and open platform for FPGA prototyping and development. 
The emergence of cocotb, a Python-based verification framework, opens the door to more flexible and high-level verification workflows. 
This thesis uses the IIC-OSIC-TOOLS suite, including cocotb, to develop and apply a complete verification workflow for a Verilog-based LED driver, and deploys the verified design on the GateMate FPGA Evaluation Board from Cologne Chip AG.
